text: The surgeon's left hand grasps the tissue at the area of demarcation, highlighting the boundary between different tissue regions.
The anvil is placed through the fan and steel incision, which will later be used for specimen extraction.
The surgeon proceeds to divide the bowel, observing a color change that indicates adequate perfusion despite the inability to use ICG due to technical difficulties.

The surgeon places a clip to the left of the stapler, indicating the area of pulsatile blood flow. The proximal margin of the specimen is identified, and the surgeon proceeds to divide the specimen using the stapler.
The surgeon closes the colotomy to prevent spillage of fecal matter or tumor cells during specimen extraction in a cancer case.
The surgeon uses a stapler to divide the distal section of the colon, with a noticeable color change in the tissue indicating the transition between different segments.

The surgeon places the EEA stapler trans-anally, carefully pulling the anterior staple line posteriorly to avoid crossing staple lines. The spike of the stapler is brought out, with no concern for ischemia in the small portion of tissue between the staple line and the circular stapler.
